subtitle: Latent Dirichlet Allocation over the port-digitalization literature
description: Thematic mapping of 125 full-text peer-reviewed articles on Port Community Systems, maritime single windows and smart ports.
exec_intro: This report presents a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic model of 125 full-text academic articles (2007–2026) on the digitalization of seaports — Port Community Systems (PCS), maritime single windows, smart ports and related themes. Following a C_V coherence sweep over k = 3–20 and a structured interpretability assessment, a twelve-topic solution was selected. The twelve topics are:

key_findings.text: The documentary backbone of the literature is formed by Port Community Systems for import/export flows (T8) together with the two single-window streams (T6, T7): jointly they account for roughly a third of the corpus and dominate the 2009–2016 period, when EU reporting-formalities directives and national single-window programmes drove the research agenda.

Governance and sustainable digital transformation (T10) is the largest single cluster (22 dominant articles, 16.5% prevalence) and rises steadily from 2017 onwards, while cybersecurity (T1) and bibliometric research-trend mapping (T4) concentrate almost entirely in 2024–2026 — the newest fronts of the field.

Blockchain for trade documentation (T5) forms a small but sharply delineated cluster concentrated around 2020–2023, consistent with a technology-enthusiasm wave that has since receded; platform business models (T11) remain a two-article niche connecting the field to the platform-economy literature.

introduction: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (Blei, Ng and Jordan, 2003) is a generative probabilistic model that represents each document as a mixture of latent topics and each topic as a probability distribution over terms. Applied to a scientific corpus, it provides a reproducible, corpus-wide map of the thematic structure of a literature, complementing manual review with an unsupervised, quantitative account of which research streams exist, how large they are and how they evolve over time (Ferdinand et al., 2024). The objective of this analysis is to identify the latent thematic structure of a 125-article corpus on seaport digitalization — Port Community Systems, maritime single windows, smart ports and adjacent streams — in order to structure the literature review of the associated dissertation, assign each article to its dominant research stream, and characterize the temporal and geographic contours of the field.
corpus_paragraph: The corpus comprises 125 peer-reviewed journal articles and conference papers published between 2007 and 2026, collected for the dissertation's literature review. Full text was extracted from the PDF files with PyMuPDF. Extraction was followed by structural cleaning: reference lists were removed (cut at the last 'References'/'Bibliography' heading in the second half of each document); per-page furniture (repeated headers, footers and download/copyright stamps) was dropped; hyphenation across line breaks was repaired; in-text citations (parenthetical author–year strings and 'Author et al.' mentions) and URLs were removed; and non-English passages in three bilingual journals were filtered out. Table 1 summarizes the corpus and model configuration.

preprocessing_bullets.text: Lower-casing and tokenization retaining alphabetic tokens with at least three characters.

Standard English stopwords (NLTK) extended with an iteratively built academic/domain list (e.g., paper, study, journal, publisher names) and with Spanish, Portuguese, French, Italian and German stopword lists as a safeguard for residual non-English text.
spaCy (en_core_web_sm) lemmatization, with post-correction of acronym plurals (e.g., PCSs → pcs) and of the 'data' lemma.

Statistically salient bigrams and trigrams detected with the gensim Phrases model (min_count = 5, threshold = 10), so that multiword expressions such as port_community, single_window, smart_port, bill_lading and digital_transformation are treated as single terms.

dictionary_paragraph: The preprocessed tokens were mapped to a gensim dictionary, filtered by removing terms occurring in fewer than 5 documents or in more than 50% of documents (no_below = 5, no_above = 0.5), following Ferdinand et al. (2024). The filter yields 5,002 terms and, importantly, removes corpus-wide vocabulary (port, system, data, information), which forces the model to discriminate documents on more specific terminology. Each document was then encoded as a bag-of-words vector (doc2bow).
inference_paragraph: Topics were inferred with gensim's LdaModel, which implements online Variational Bayes. The final model uses k = 12 topics, a symmetric document–topic prior alpha = 0.1, a topic–word prior beta (eta) = 0.01, 20 passes over the corpus with 400 inference iterations per document batch (chunksize = 64). Five random initializations (seeds 42, 7, 13, 21, 99) were trained; the reported model fixes seed 42, selected through a structured interpretability comparison of the candidate solutions, whose C_V (0.4833) lies within one standard deviation of the best initialization (0.4971). Fixing the seed makes the reported model exactly reproducible from the accompanying scripts.
k_selection_paragraph: The number of topics k is the main analyst-controlled parameter of LDA. It was selected with a coherence sweep: for each k from 3 to 20, three models with different random seeds were trained and their C_V coherence (Röder, Both and Hinneburg, 2015) computed against the corpus; C_V correlates best with human judgements of topic quality among the standard coherence measures. Following the heuristic of Ferdinand et al. (2024) — choose the highest coherence before the curve flattens or drops — the sweep was complemented by a qualitative interpretability assessment of the candidate solutions, since coherence alone is a guiding metric rather than a decision rule.
selection_justification: Figure 1 shows the sweep. Mean C_V rises steeply up to k ≈ 10, reaches a plateau at k = 10–12 and then oscillates within noise up to k = 20. The global maximum sits at k = 14 (C_V = 0.5028), but with the highest seed variance of the sweep (± 0.0286), while k = 12 attains a statistically indistinguishable coherence (0.4920) with the lowest variance (± 0.0075) — the most reproducible solution. A structured qualitative comparison of the k = 10, 12 and 14 solutions across seeds confirmed the choice of k = 12: at k = 10 distinct streams remain merged (governance with platform business models; terminal operations never separates), while at k = 14 the extra capacity fragments into single-case shards and unlabelable residual topics. The twelve-topic solution offers the cleanest separation of the field's recognized research streams — single-window, PCS operations, blockchain, cybersecurity, governance/sustainability, smart-port evaluation — while every topic remains labelable.
results_explainer: Each topic is a probability distribution over the 5,002 dictionary terms, and each article is a mixture of topics. Topic labels were assigned by joint inspection of the highest-probability terms and of the articles most strongly associated with each topic. Figure 2 shows the dominant terms of the twelve topics; Table 3 summarizes their size (number of articles for which the topic is dominant) and prevalence (mean share of the topic across all 125 documents). The subsections below present the topics in descending order of prevalence.

0.label: Port call coordination and inter-organizational information sharing
0.interpretation: Topic T0 gathers work on the coordination of port calls and the inter-organizational information exchange that underpins them, as reflected in its leading terms (port_call, information_sharing, barrier, berth, arrival, coordinate). Its dominant articles centre on how digital systems align the actions of shipping companies, terminals and port managers around vessel arrivals, ranging from Just-in-Time arrival and berth allocation policies (e.g., Mubder, 2024; Pahl, 2024) to the mechanisms sustaining continued use of Port Community Systems (e.g., Mubder and Fredriksson, 2025) and the effect of port-centric information integration on performance (Jiang et al., 2023). What unites these studies is a process-oriented view of digitalization: technology matters insofar as it enables timely, shared information among the many actors involved in a single port call, while institutional and organizational barriers condition whether that coordination materializes (Gustafsson, 2007; Fedi et al., 2019). With eleven dominant articles spanning 2007 to 2025, this constitutes a mid-sized but core stream of the corpus, bridging the operational literature on berth and arrival management with the broader Port Community System adoption debate.

1.label: Cybersecurity and safety of digital maritime infrastructure
1.interpretation: Anchored by terms such as attack, threat, vulnerability, detection, and cybersecurity alongside safety, compliance, and regulatory, T1 captures the research stream concerned with securing and safeguarding the digital infrastructure on which port and maritime operations increasingly depend. Its most strongly loading documents address cyber risk directly, from a discrete-event-systems survey of maritime and port cybersecurity (e.g., Liu et al., 2025) to threat and security models for cross-border paperless trade systems (Kayisoglu et al., 2025) and an early investment analysis of information security management in Croatian seaports (Aksentijević et al., 2012). The remaining articles broaden the frame from deliberate attacks to the dependability and regulatory soundness of digital infrastructure more generally, spanning container-handling systems, customs legal frameworks, and maritime logbooks as strategic infrastructure. What unites the cluster is a shared view of digitalization as a source of exposure as well as capability, treating risk assessment, incident detection, and compliance as prerequisites for trustworthy port systems. Within the corpus this is a secondary but clearly delineated stream rather than a core one, and its concentration of very recent publications suggests a theme that is gaining momentum as ports' digital dependence deepens.

2.label: Operational optimization, resilience and service quality
2.interpretation: A smaller, methods-oriented cluster, T2 gathers quantitative modeling work in which digitalization is examined through its effects on port operational performance, resilience, and service quality, as reflected in top terms such as optimization, modeling, variable, berth, disruption, and container_terminal. One strand comprises formal optimization and simulation frameworks — an event-driven real-time tactical-operational optimization framework for smart ports (e.g., Abril et al., 2024), a computational Port Community System framework for synchromodal freight networks (e.g., Nabais et al., 2018), and resilient design of hyperconnected Physical Internet supply networks (Tordecilla et al., 2025) — while a second strand uses construct-based survey models linking information systems use and digital adoption to perceived service quality and business sustainability, notably in Indonesian ports (e.g., Bakari and Subriadi, 2020; Utama et al., 2024), which explains the prominence of the indonesia term. What unites these otherwise methodologically diverse papers is a shared analytical posture: digital systems are treated as measurable inputs to models of port efficiency, resilience to disruption, and service outcomes rather than as institutional or governance phenomena. With only a handful of dominant documents and a modest share of the corpus, this is a niche, somewhat heterogeneous stream at the margins of the collection, complementing the larger adoption- and governance-focused topics with a performance-modeling perspective.

3.label: Digital innovation adoption: barriers, stakeholders and maturity
3.interpretation: Topic T3 captures the socio-organizational side of port digitalization: research on the barriers, stakeholder dynamics, and maturity of digital innovation adoption, as signalled by top terms such as barrier, terminal_operator, ict, edi, success, power, and maturity_model. Its dominant articles examine why inter-organizational systems succeed or stall, ranging from barriers and facilitators of digital innovation and cost-benefit framings of PCS competitiveness (e.g., Carlan et al., 2016, 2017) to knowledge-management obstacles in PCS-enabled ports (Zerbino et al., 2018) and maturity models for smart ports (Boullauazan et al., 2023). What unites the cluster is a shift of analytical focus from the technology itself to the actors around it — terminal operators, shipping lines, and shippers — whose interests, power relations, and political dynamics shape adoption, a thread made explicit in studies of de-politicization at Tema (Aryee & Hansen, 2022) and of power mutation in Piraeus (Makris & Vradis, 2024). Within the corpus this constitutes a mid-sized but clearly delineated core stream, providing the behavioural and institutional counterpart to the more technical architecture- and platform-oriented topics.

4.label: Research trends and bibliometric mapping of port digitalization
4.interpretation: Rather than a substantive application domain, topic T4 gathers the corpus's meta-scientific layer: bibliometric analyses, systematic reviews, and research-trend mapping of port digitalization, as signalled by top terms such as "publication," "cluster," "trend," "academic," "theme," and "systematic" alongside the technology buzzwords ("big_data," "iot," "smart_port," "sensor") that these studies track. Its dominant documents survey the field itself rather than a particular system, ranging from analyses of maritime technology attention cycles and buzzword stability (e.g., Jalali & Tei, 2025) to a structured literature review of Port Community Systems (Moros-Daza et al., 2020) and a systematic review of artificial intelligence in seaport smart-gate processes (Teixeira et al., 2026). What unites these articles is a shared review methodology — searching, clustering, and thematizing prior publications to chart where scholarly attention on smart ports and maritime logistics is heading — rather than a common empirical object. With only four dominant articles and a modest share of corpus attention, T4 is a small, clearly niche cluster, and it is somewhat heterogeneous at the margins (Lai, 2025, on data-fusion-driven collaborative logistics, sits less squarely in the review genre); nonetheless, it plays a useful reflexive role by consolidating and orienting the substantive streams captured by the other topics.

5.label: Blockchain for trade documentation and port community systems
5.interpretation: Sharply delineated by its vocabulary, this cluster captures the literature on distributed-ledger technology as an alternative or complement to conventional port community systems for handling trade documentation: the dominant terms (blockchain, smart_contract, node, bill_lading, ethereum, and the TradeLens platform) point to technically oriented work on decentralized architectures for document exchange among sellers, freight forwarders, and port actors. Representative contributions include architectural design requirements for a blockchain-based PCS (e.g., Irannezhad, 2020), the use of IoT and blockchain to address bill-of-lading issues (Irannezhad and Faroqi, 2023), and a conceptual comparison of PCS and blockchain scenarios for maritime document handling (Tsiulin et al., 2020), alongside proposals extending the technology to interport communities (Serra et al., 2022). What unites these articles is a shared focus on trust, disintermediation, and smart-contract automation in cross-organizational documentary flows, typically at the level of design proposals, prototypes, and feasibility assessments rather than large-scale deployments. Within the corpus this is a comparatively small but highly coherent and technology-specific stream, reflecting a wave of blockchain enthusiasm concentrated around 2020-2023 rather than a core pillar of the port-digitalization literature.

6.label: National maritime single window: architecture and implementation
6.interpretation: Topic T6 captures the design and national implementation of maritime single window environments and their integration with Port Community Systems, as signalled by terms such as nmsw, nsw, module, imo, reporting, clearance, and vts. Its dominant articles are largely case-based accounts of building these platforms in specific national contexts, particularly Croatia and Montenegro (e.g., Tijan et al., 2018; Kapidani et al., 2021; Aksentijevic et al., 2022), alongside taxonomic and architectural contributions such as Rødseth and Kapidani (2017) and Hagaseth et al. (2023). What unites these studies is a system-engineering and implementation perspective: they specify modular architectures, prototypes, risk-management approaches, and cost-benefit assessments for harmonizing ship reporting formalities and administrative clearance through a single national entry point, often under IMO and EU regulatory impetus. The strong presence of Adriatic and small-coastal-state cases (Rijeka, Split, Bar, Taranto) indicates that this stream is heavily shaped by a regional research community documenting successive stages of the same digitalization programmes. With one of the largest sets of dominant documents in the corpus, T6 constitutes a core stream, representing the institutional-infrastructure backbone of seaport digitalization research.

7.label: Single-window trade facilitation: national implementation cases
7.interpretation: Anchored by terms such as "msw", "trade_facilitation", "agency", "administration", and country markers like "montenegro", "turkey", and "india", this stream gathers country-level case studies of maritime single window implementation as an instrument of trade facilitation and administrative reform (e.g., Beškovnik, 2015; Keceli, 2011; Peynirci, 2023). Its documents typically examine how national administrations and port communities in specific settings—the Adriatic region, Turkey, Montenegro's Port of Bar, India, and Indonesia (Yusuf, 2023)—introduce single-window platforms to streamline document submission across agencies, reduce clearance times, and align with international trade law. What unites them is a policy-and-implementation orientation: rather than proposing generic architectures, they evaluate concrete national deployments and their institutional prerequisites, with the terms "reduction", "hour", and "estimate" reflecting a recurring concern with quantified efficiency gains. The cluster is nonetheless somewhat heterogeneous at its margins, absorbing data-space and cognitive-platform contributions (e.g., Sarabia-Jácome et al., 2020; Belsa et al., 2023) that share the emphasis on inter-agency data exchange. With a dominant-article count and prevalence close to the corpus average, it constitutes a solid mid-sized stream that complements the more architecture-focused single-window topic (T6) with an implementation and trade-policy perspective.

8.label: Port community systems for import/export documentation flows
8.interpretation: Anchored by the corpus's most PCS-specific vocabulary (pcs, export, shipment, import, custom_declaration, freight_forwarder, portbase), Topic T8 captures the operational core of Port Community System research: how electronic platforms streamline import/export documentation and cargo information flows across the port logistics chain. Its dominant articles concentrate on efficiency and process improvement in concrete port settings, from integrated information flows and service-oriented PCS modeling (e.g., Bisogno et al., 2015; Nota et al., 2018) to the effect of PCS characteristics on port performance (Caldeirinha et al., 2020) and group-specific process improvements in Rotterdam (Simoni et al., 2022), with a notable Italian and broader European case orientation. What unites these studies is a shared framing of the PCS as a documentary and logistical hub whose value is measured in reduced processing times and smoother coordination among forwarders, customs and terminal actors, extending more recently toward Physical Internet and track-and-trace architectures (Fahim et al., 2021). Spanning 2009 to 2025 and accounting for one of the largest shares of the corpus, this is unambiguously a core stream — arguably the thematic backbone of the PCS literature reviewed here.

9.label: Smart-port evaluation: indicators, KPIs and adoption strategies
9.interpretation: Topic T9 gathers the evaluative and decision-analytic strand of smart-port research, in which digitalization is assessed through indicators, key performance indicators, composite indices and expert-weighted criteria (terms such as smart_port, indicator, kpi, evaluation, ahp, expert). Its dominant articles build measurement frameworks and prioritization models: performance evaluation of container terminal operation systems (e.g., Liu et al., 2022), identification of key success factors for smart ports in the post-pandemic era (Hsu et al., 2023), optimal smart-technology adoption strategies under network externalities (Li et al., 2023), and multi-criteria ranking of resilience drivers in Chilean ports (González-Solano et al., 2025). What unites these studies is a shared methodological orientation — multi-criteria decision-making, expert elicitation and dashboard- or index-based assessment — applied to the question of how "smart" a port is and which investments or strategies should be prioritized. That the stream reaches back to early performance evaluation of Port Community System implementation (Tsamboulas et al., 2012) shows continuity between older PCS appraisal work and current smart-port benchmarking. Within the corpus this constitutes a coherent mid-sized stream rather than a core one: smaller than the dominant PCS and governance topics, but clearly delineated by its quantitative-evaluative focus.

10.label: Governance and sustainable digital transformation of ports
10.interpretation: Anchored by the terms governance, sustainability, digital_transformation, ecosystem, and public_private, this stream captures research that frames port digitalization as an institutional and strategic transformation rather than a purely technical one. Its dominant articles range from studies of port authority reform and the governance of inter-organizational information sharing (e.g., de Langen & van der Lugt, 2017; Klievink et al., 2012; Tijan et al., 2021) to assessment frameworks for digital maturity, smart-port indices, and port sustainability (e.g., Franchi et al., 2025; Molavi et al., 2020). What unites these contributions is a management- and policy-level lens: digital transformation is analyzed through the governance capacity of port authorities, public-private collaboration, and its alignment with sustainability and energy-transition objectives, frequently operationalized via maturity models, indicators, and comparative regional assessments. As the largest cluster in the corpus, it constitutes the conceptual core of the field, linking the more technically oriented topics on platforms, single windows, and smart-port technologies to broader questions of institutional design and sustainable development.

11.label: Digital-platform business models in freight transport
11.interpretation: Occupying the margins of the corpus, T11 captures a small, methodologically distinctive stream that applies taxonomy development to digital-platform business models in freight transport, as signalled by the dominant terms taxonomy, business_model, dimension, cluster, and digital_platform alongside marketplace, value_creation, and governance. Its two dominant articles—Bossong et al. (2025) on emerging business models of digital platforms in intermodal freight transport, and Teßmann and Elbert (2022) on a taxonomy of multi-sided B2B platforms under varying governmental influence—share a common design-science logic: classifying platform ventures along dimensions such as value proposition, geographic scope, profit orientation, and governance arrangements. What unites them is the treatment of port community systems and freight marketplaces not as IT artefacts but as platform businesses whose ecosystems and value-creation mechanisms can be systematically typologized. With only two dominant documents and a marginal share of the corpus, this is clearly a niche, borderline residual cluster rather than a core research stream, yet it usefully connects the port digitalization literature to the broader platform-economy and business-model scholarship.

temporal_interpretation: The temporal pattern in Figure 4 traces the field's maturation. The documentary-infrastructure streams dominate the first decade: PCS import/export flows (T8) peak between 2009 and 2016, and the two single-window topics (T6, T7) surge from 2011 to 2016, in the wake of Directive 2010/65/EU on reporting formalities and the associated national implementation programmes. From 2017 onwards the centre of gravity shifts to the strategic-institutional layer: governance and sustainable digital transformation (T10) grows steadily and holds the largest share of the 2024–2026 period, while cybersecurity (T1) and bibliometric mapping (T4) emerge as the newest streams, concentrated in the last three years. Blockchain (T5) exhibits a clear wave pattern centred on 2020–2023. Port-call coordination (T0), whose lone 2007 article makes the earliest column, re-emerges in the 2020s around Just-in-Time port calls — an old coordination problem revisited with new digital instruments.
temporal_note: The 2007–2011 columns contain a single article each and 2026 is a partial year of in-press items (see Figure 5), so single documents fully determine those cells; shares before 2012 should be read as illustrative only.
geographic_paragraph: China (27 articles), the United States (26) and Singapore (23) are the most frequently mentioned countries, reflecting their role as reference cases of port scale and digital maturity. The next tier is distinctly European — Italy, the United Kingdom, Croatia, the Netherlands, Germany and Spain — mirroring the corpus's strong orientation toward EU-funded PCS and single-window case studies; the prominence of Croatia (17) and of the Adriatic cluster more broadly matches the single-window implementation streams (T6, T7), much of whose literature documents Croatian and Montenegrin national programmes. Colombia (11) and Indonesia (10) anchor the Latin-American and Southeast-Asian case-study strands. Note that counts measure mentions in article bodies (at least two mentions per article), a proxy for geographic focus rather than for author affiliation.

robustness_bullets.text: All results derive from deterministic scripts with fixed random seeds (final model: seed 42, alpha = 0.1, eta = 0.01, k = 12); the full pipeline — extraction, preprocessing, sweep, model, figures, report — can be re-run end-to-end from the accompanying code.

k was chosen where the coherence plateau begins (k = 12: C_V = 0.4920 ± 0.0075, the lowest seed variance of the sweep) and validated by a structured interpretability comparison against k = 10 and k = 14; the final initialization was selected among five seeds for interpretability, with coherence within one standard deviation of the best seed.

limitations_bullets.text: Topic solutions vary across random initializations: the mean adjusted Rand index between the dominant-topic partitions of the five seeds is 0.135, and the mean top-10 term overlap of matched topics is 34%. The reported topics are one defensible reading of the corpus, not a unique decomposition; document-level assignments (Appendix A) should be read as probabilistic tendencies.

Modeling full texts (rather than abstracts) captures richer signal but also methods vocabulary and case-specific place names; despite aggressive cleaning, topics T2 and T7 retain visible case-study and survey-methods flavor, and two topics (T4, T11) rest on four or fewer dominant articles.

C_V coherence is a guiding heuristic, not an optimality criterion: the k = 10–20 plateau means several k values are defensible, and the final choice of k = 12 rests partly on qualitative interpretability judgements.

Three bilingual articles had their non-English text filtered; the earliest years (2007–2011) contribute one article each; and 2026 is incomplete — temporal readings at the edges of the window are correspondingly fragile.

conclusions: The LDA decomposition organizes the 125-article corpus into three readable layers. A documentary-regulatory layer — PCS import/export flows (T8), single-window architecture and implementation (T6, T7) and blockchain documentation (T5) — constitutes the historical core of the field and reflects two decades of effort to dematerialize trade and reporting formalities. A technical-operational layer — port-call coordination (T0), operational optimization and service quality (T2), smart-port evaluation (T9) and cybersecurity (T1) — treats digitalization as an instrument of, and a risk to, port performance. A strategic-institutional layer — adoption barriers and stakeholder dynamics (T3), governance and sustainable digital transformation (T10), platform business models (T11) and research-trend mapping (T4) — frames digitalization as an organizational and policy transformation. Temporally, the corpus documents a clear trajectory from the documentary layer (dominant 2009–2016) toward the strategic-institutional one (dominant since 2017), with cybersecurity and meta-scientific reflection as the newest fronts. For the dissertation, the twelve topics provide both a defensible structure for the literature-review chapters and an empirical map of where the field's open questions — governance capacity, sustainability alignment, cyber-resilience — are currently concentrated.
appendixA_explainer: The table below lists, for each of the 125 articles, its dominant topic (the topic with the highest share in the article's topic mixture) and the corresponding proportion. Articles are sorted by topic and, within each topic, by descending proportion. Topic labels: T0 — Port call coordination and inter-organizational information sharing; T1 — Cybersecurity and safety of digital maritime infrastructure; T2 — Operational optimization, resilience and service quality; T3 — Digital innovation adoption: barriers, stakeholders and maturity; T4 — Research trends and bibliometric mapping of port digitalization; T5 — Blockchain for trade documentation and port community systems; T6 — National maritime single window: architecture and implementation; T7 — Single-window trade facilitation: national implementation cases; T8 — Port community systems for import/export documentation flows; T9 — Smart-port evaluation: indicators, KPIs and adoption strategies; T10 — Governance and sustainable digital transformation of ports; T11 — Digital-platform business models in freight transport.
appendixB_explainer: The matrix below gives the full document–topic distribution of the final model (minimum_probability = 0): each row is an article, each column a topic, each cell the topic's share of that article; rows sum to 1 and the dominant cell is set in bold. Studies are sorted alphabetically.
